hints: The longer piece is L=max(X,1−X) with X~U(0,1).
solution: The longer piece $L=\max(X,1-X)$ ranges over $(\tfrac12,1)$ and is uniformly distributed there (each value $\ell$ comes from $X=\ell$ or $X=1-\ell$). A uniform on an interval of length $\tfrac12$ has variance $\dfrac{(1/2)^2}{12}=\dfrac1{48}.$

hints: Find the fixed point of this affine recursion.
solution: The recursion is $p_i=p\,p_{i-1}+(1-p)(1-p_{i-1})$. A fixed point $L$ satisfies $L=pL+(1-p)(1-L)$, i.e. $2L(1-p)=1-p$, giving $L=\tfrac12$. Since $|2p-1|<1$ the recursion converges, so $\lim p_n=\tfrac12.$

hints: Condition on nobody picking 7: all picks from {1..6}, 6⁷ equally likely.
7 is the ONLY missing value ⟺ the 7 picks cover all of {1..6} (a surjection).
solution: Given nobody picked $7$, the $7$ picks are uniform over $\{1,\dots,6\}$ ($6^7$ outcomes). For $7$ to be the only missing value, every value $1$–$6$ must appear, i.e. the picks form a surjection onto $6$ values — one value used twice, the rest once: $\binom72\cdot6!=15120$ ways. So $P=\dfrac{15120}{6^7}=\dfrac{35}{648}.$

hints: Independent BMs contribute 0 covariance; only the shared B₂ matters.
solution: Since $B_1,B_2,B_3$ are independent, only the common $B_2$ term contributes: $\mathrm{Cov}(B_2(1),B_2(2))=\min(1,2)=1$. All cross terms vanish, so the covariance is $1.$

hints: Let A,B,C be the three block sums (each variance 50), independent.
solution: Let $A,B,C$ be the sums over indices $1$–$50$, $51$–$100$, $101$–$150$, each with variance $50$ and mutually independent. Then $U=3A+B$, $V=B+3C$, so $\mathrm{Cov}(U,V)=\mathrm{Var}(B)=50$ and $\mathrm{Var}(U)=\mathrm{Var}(V)=9(50)+50=500$. Hence $\mathrm{Corr}=\dfrac{50}{500}=\dfrac1{10}.$
With $k=t/54$, population $=2\cdot4^{k}=2^{2k+1}$ and capacity $=16384\cdot2^{k}=2^{14+k}$. Equating exponents, $2k+1=14+k\Rightarrow k=13$, so $t=13\cdot54=702$ years.

hints: Re-spinning makes each pull an independent 1/6 chance of firing.
You win if the friend fires on his turn before you fire on yours.
solution: With a re-spin each turn, every pull fires with probability $\tfrac16$ independently. You win if the friend (who goes first) fires before you do: $q=\tfrac16+\big(\tfrac56\big)^2 q$ (friend safe and you safe returns to start). So $q\big(1-\tfrac{25}{36}\big)=\tfrac16\Rightarrow q=\tfrac{6}{11}.$

hints: Hold x stock units and y down-bets; up pays 100x−100y, down pays −50x+100y.
Minimal integers x=4,y=3 give a guaranteed 100x−100y=100.
solution: Buy $x$ stock units and place $y$ casino bets on a decline. Up: $100x-100y$; down: $-50x+100y$. For a riskless profit set these equal: $100x-100y=-50x+100y\Rightarrow 3x=4y$. The smallest integers are $x=4,y=3$ (7 units total), yielding a guaranteed profit of $100\cdot4-100\cdot3=\$100$ in either outcome.

hints: Condition on the first goat's gender.
After a male, waiting for a female is Geom(1/4) (mean 4); after a female, Geom(3/4) (mean 4/3).
Both first-heads times are Geom(1/3); use symmetry plus the tie probability.
solution: Both wait Geom$(\tfrac13)$ for the first head. The probability they tie is $\sum_{k\ge1}\big(\tfrac23\big)^{2(k-1)}\big(\tfrac13\big)^2=\tfrac{1/9}{1-4/9}=\tfrac15$. By symmetry $P(\text{Bill}<\text{Bob})=\tfrac{1-1/5}{2}=\tfrac25.$

hints: Put–call parity on a future: P − C = K − F (undiscounted here).
solution: For options on a future, $P-C=K-F$ (ignoring discounting), so $P=C+(K-F)=3+(120-110)=13.$
